title: Week 4: Social-Emotional Foundation
parent_frame: This week, name every feeling — yours and theirs — out loud.
why_this_matters: The toddler years are the crucible of emotional development. Your child now has desires, opinions, and will — but not yet the capacity to manage any of them. What adults call "tantrums" are what the child experiences as being overwhelmed by feelings too big for their nervous system. Your job is not to stop the feelings. It is to name them, validate them, and hold the boundary. This is how self-regulation is built — not through control, but through a calm, consistent presence that shows the child their emotions have names and their emotions are survivable. This is the operating system Phase 1 assumes is installed.
focus_items: Name emotions in real time: "You're frustrated," "You're excited," "You're sad"
Use the three-part formula: name it, validate it, hold the limit
Model sharing and turn-taking with language: "My turn... your turn"
Teach "use your words" by giving them the words first
Stay calm during meltdowns — your regulation IS the lesson
Allow safe frustration: do not rescue immediately
keep_doing: Daily outdoor time · Consistent sleep & meal routines · Let them try before helping · Read aloud 2-3x daily · Name emotions in real time · "Please" and "thank you" modeled constantly
what_you_need: Your calm. Your patience. A few board books about feelings (optional but helpful). Nothing else.

daily_moments.say_this: Good morning! How are you feeling? Are you happy to be awake? Or are you still sleepy? It's okay to feel sleepy. Sleepy is a feeling. Let's get dressed — do you want the green shirt or the striped shirt? You want the striped shirt. Good — you chose. Oh, you're frustrated with the sleeve? I see that. It's hard when your arm won't go through. Take a breath. Try again. You did it! That felt good, didn't it?
do_this: Ask how they feel when they wake up — name what you see if they cannot answer
If they resist getting dressed, name the feeling: "You don't want to get dressed. I understand. We still need to."
When they struggle with clothing, let them feel the frustration briefly before helping
Celebrate effort, not just success: "You tried so hard on that zipper"
daily_moments.what_this_builds: Emotion vocabulary at day's start, frustration tolerance, recovery after struggle, feeling-word fluency

daily_moments.say_this: Time for a diaper change. I know — you don't want to stop playing. You're disappointed. You were having fun with the trucks. I understand. We'll come right back to the trucks. Can you help me? Thank you for lying down. Thank you for helping. That was hard to stop playing, and you did it anyway. I appreciate that.
do_this: Name the resistance: "You're upset because you had to stop"
Validate without caving: "I know you don't want to. We need to do it."
Thank them for cooperating — even reluctant cooperation
Use this as a mini-practice in handling unwanted transitions
Stay calm and matter-of-fact, even if they resist physically
daily_moments.what_this_builds: Naming feelings about unwanted events, accepting necessary tasks, cooperation through validation, transition tolerance

daily_moments.say_this: Here's your lunch. I see you're excited about the pasta! You love pasta. Can you say 'more, please'? 'More, please' — thank you! Oh, you don't want the peas. That's okay — you don't have to eat them. But they stay on the plate. You're angry because you wanted more bread? I hear you. You're angry. The answer is: after you try one bite of chicken. That's the deal. You tried it! Thank you. Here's your bread.
do_this: Name their mealtime emotions: excitement, disgust, frustration, impatience
Practice "please" and "thank you" at every request and every serving
Hold limits calmly: "I know you want more crackers. First, try the chicken."
Model turn-taking: "It's your turn to pick something from the plate. Now it's my turn."
If they throw food in frustration, stay calm: "I see you're upset. We don't throw food. You can say 'all done.'"
Give them words for what they want: "You can say 'no thank you' instead of pushing the plate"
daily_moments.what_this_builds: Emotion naming during daily routine, social language (please/thank you), handling limits without collapse, using words instead of actions

daily_moments.say_this: Look — there's another child at the park! Can you wave? Wave and say hi. Do you want to play near them? You're feeling shy? That's okay. Shy is a feeling. We can watch first. Oh, they want to use the slide and you're on it. It's their turn now. Your turn is done. I know — you want more turns. You can wait, and then you'll get another turn. Waiting is hard. You're doing it.
do_this: Name social emotions: shy, excited, nervous, brave
Coach sharing and turn-taking in real time: "Your turn, then their turn"
Model greeting other children: wave, say hi, say your name
If a conflict happens, narrate both sides: "You want the swing. She wants the swing. We need to take turns."
If they get hurt or scared, name it: "That hurt. You're scared. I'm here."
Celebrate brave moments: "You went down the big slide! You were nervous and you did it anyway."
daily_moments.what_this_builds: Social-emotional awareness with peers, turn-taking in real situations, courage and risk-taking, empathy for others' feelings

daily_moments.say_this: Oh no, the tower fell down! How do you feel? Frustrated? Mad? It's frustrating when something you built falls down. I understand. Do you want to try again? You can feel mad AND try again. Those two things can happen at the same time. Look — you're building it back up. You didn't give up. That's what strong people do. They feel frustrated, and then they try again.
do_this: Let them fail — do not fix the tower, catch the block, or prevent the crash
When frustration hits, name it and wait: "You're frustrated. I see that."
Do not say "it's okay" — say "that IS frustrating" (validate the feeling, do not dismiss it)
Model your own emotions: "Oops, I dropped it. I feel frustrated too. Let me try again."
Practice taking turns: "I'll stack one, you stack one. My turn... your turn."
If they hit or throw in anger: "You're angry. I understand. You cannot hit. You can say 'I'm angry.'"
daily_moments.what_this_builds: Frustration tolerance, recovery after failure, emotion-to-language connection, modeling self-regulation, anger without aggression

daily_moments.say_this: Bath time! I know you don't want to stop playing. You're disappointed. That's okay. Playing was fun, and now it's bath time. Can you help pick — bubbles or no bubbles tonight? You chose bubbles! That made you happy. I can see your smile. Happy is a good feeling. Now can you share the cup with me? I pour, then you pour. My turn... your turn. Thank you for sharing.
do_this: Name the transition emotion: "You're sad to stop playing. I understand."
Once in the bath, name positive emotions too: joy, excitement, silliness
Practice turn-taking with bath toys: "Your turn to pour, my turn to pour"
If they splash wildly and get water in their eyes: "That surprised you! Surprised is a feeling."
If they resist getting out: "You love the bath. It's hard to stop. The bath is done. We can have bath time again tomorrow."
daily_moments.what_this_builds: Transition emotions, positive emotion naming (not just distress), turn-taking, handling endings and disappointment

daily_moments.say_this: We're almost done playing. In a few minutes, it's time to clean up and eat dinner. I know — you want to keep playing. You can feel sad about stopping AND still help clean up. Can you put the cars in the basket? Thank you. I appreciate you helping even when you didn't want to stop. That's hard to do, and you did it.
do_this: Give a warning before transitions: "In two minutes, we're cleaning up"
Name their feeling about the transition: "You don't want to stop. I understand."
Validate AND hold: "I know. It's still time to clean up."
Thank them for cooperating through disappointment — this is a big deal
If a meltdown happens at transition, stay calm, stay present, wait it out, then return to the task
Never skip the transition because of the meltdown — that teaches melting down gets results
daily_moments.what_this_builds: Handling unwanted transitions, emotional flexibility, cooperating through displeasure, the separation of feeling from action

daily_moments.say_this: Let's read a book before bed. How about this one? Look at this character — how does she feel? She looks sad. Why do you think she's sad? Because her friend left. That would make me sad too. Have you ever felt sad? You felt sad when Grandma left. I remember. Sad is a big feeling. But Grandma came back, didn't she? Okay, let's read more. Good night, sweet one. I love you. You had big feelings today, and you handled them.
do_this: Choose books that show characters with emotions (almost any picture book works)
Ask "how does he feel?" and "why do you think?" — wait for any answer
Connect book emotions to their real experiences: "You felt that way too, remember?"
Name your own emotions: "This part makes me feel happy"
End the day with affirmation: "You had big feelings and you were brave"
Keep the bedtime routine identical every night — predictability is emotional safety
daily_moments.what_this_builds: Empathy through story, connecting fiction to personal experience, emotional vocabulary review, secure closure to the day

reflection_questions: Emotions they named on their own this week
How they handled the biggest meltdown
A moment they showed empathy or kindness
The transition that was hardest for them
[ ] Screens stayed under _____ min/day

title: Week 5: Routine & Habit Formation
parent_frame: This week, make the rhythm visible — and give them a role in it.
why_this_matters: The daily routine is not a schedule imposed on the child — it is the environment their nervous system learns to trust. A child who has lived inside a predictable rhythm since infancy arrives at Phase 1 and finds IC1.1-A1's visual schedule and habit architecture feel like a natural extension of what they already know. A child who has lived in chaos experiences it as an alien imposition. Consistency is not rigidity. It is the kindest thing you can give a toddler — the knowledge that the world is predictable and they have a place in it.
focus_items: Make the child a participant in every routine, not a passenger
Create or reference visual cues: a picture chart for bedtime, morning, mealtime
Maintain the same sequence every day — predictability is the goal
Begin cleanup-before-next-activity as a hard rule (with help)
Give them one small "job" they do every day without being asked
Use transition language: "First we ___, then we ___"
keep_doing: Daily outdoor time · Consistent sleep & meal routines · Let them try before helping · Read aloud 2-3x daily · Name emotions in real time · "Please" and "thank you" modeled constantly
what_you_need: Simple picture chart (hand-drawn is fine: bath, pajamas, teeth, book, bed). Consistent timing. A small daily job for the child (carrying napkins, putting shoes by the door, feeding a pet).

daily_moments.say_this: Good morning! You know what we do first when we wake up. What's first? Diaper change — that's right! Then what? Get dressed. You remembered! Let's go. After we get dressed, what comes next? Breakfast! First we get dressed, then we eat breakfast. That's our morning. Can you put your shirt on? You know how. Arms up — you're doing our morning routine.
do_this: Follow the same sequence every morning without variation
Ask "what comes next?" and let them answer (or try)
Use "first/then" language: "First we get dressed, then we eat"
Let them lead parts they know: walking to the dresser, picking clothes
If they resist, name the routine, not the command: "This is what we do in the morning"
daily_moments.what_this_builds: Routine internalization, sequencing, anticipation of what comes next, ownership of the morning

daily_moments.say_this: Diaper time comes after breakfast — and look, it's after breakfast. You knew this was coming. Can you walk to the changing table? Thank you. You know what happens — lie down, clean diaper, pants back on, then we play. You know the whole routine. What comes after diaper change? Play time! That's right. You always know what's next.
do_this: Keep diaper changes at roughly the same times each day
Narrate the predictability: "This always happens after breakfast"
Ask them to anticipate: "What comes after this?"
Let them participate: walking to the spot, handing you supplies, pulling up pants
Reinforce that they know the rhythm: "You always know what's coming next"
daily_moments.what_this_builds: Routine predictability, anticipation, participation in care sequences, trust in daily structure

daily_moments.say_this: It's lunch time! What do we do before we eat? Wash hands. That's right — we always wash hands before meals. Let's go. Now can you carry your plate to the table? Careful — hold it with two hands. You did it! Sit in your chair. When we eat, we sit at the table. When you're all done, what do you do with your plate? Carry it to the counter. That's your job. You always carry your plate.
do_this: Same sequence every meal: wash hands, sit at table, eat, carry plate, wipe face
Give them a mealtime job they own: carrying their plate, placing napkins, getting their cup
Use the same words: "We always wash hands before we eat"
Keep mealtimes at roughly the same times each day
End-of-meal routine: "All done? Plate to the counter. Thank you."
They are not helping YOU — this is THEIR job
daily_moments.what_this_builds: Mealtime routine ownership, contribution identity, consistent sequencing, role in the family rhythm

daily_moments.say_this: It's time to go outside. What do we do before we go outside? Shoes! We put our shoes on. Can you get your shoes? You know where they are — by the door. That's where shoes always go. Now put them on — you try first. Good. Now what? Open the door. Let's go! When we come back in, what do we do with shoes? Shoes go back by the door. That's the rule.
do_this: Same pre-outside routine every time: shoes on, sunscreen if needed, out the door
Same re-entry routine: shoes off, shoes by the door, wash hands
Ask them to tell you the steps before you do them
Keep outdoor time at roughly the same point in the day
If it is raining or the plan changes, name it: "Usually we go to the park. Today we're going to the backyard because it's raining. The routine is the same — shoes on, go outside."
daily_moments.what_this_builds: Pre-activity routines, coming-home routines, flexibility within structure, understanding that some things always happen even when details change

daily_moments.say_this: It's play time! But first — is your space clean? Let's look. The books from this morning are still out. What do we do before we start something new? Put the old things away. Let's do it together. Books on the shelf. Good. Now the floor is clear and you can play. What do you want to do? Blocks? Great. When you're done with blocks, what happens? Blocks go away before the next thing comes out.
do_this: Establish and enforce: clean up before starting a new activity
Say it the same way every time: "We put away what we're done with before we get something new"
Help them — this is hard and slow at this age — but make them do part of it
Do not start the new activity until cleanup is at least attempted
Name what they are learning: "You're taking care of your space"
daily_moments.what_this_builds: Cleanup-before-transition habit, task completion, responsibility for their space, sequencing (finish before starting)

daily_moments.say_this: It's bath time. You know what happens at bath time — let's go through it. First, take off clothes. Can you pull off your shirt? Now get in the tub. Now we wash — soap on arms, soap on legs, soap on belly. Then we play for a few minutes. Then we get out, dry off, and put on pajamas. You know this whole routine. What comes after bath? Pajamas! Then what? Teeth. Then what? Book! Then? Bed. You know the whole thing.
do_this: Follow the identical bath-to-bed sequence every single night
Let them narrate the sequence: "What's next? And after that?"
If you have a picture chart on the wall, point to it: "Where are we now?"
Let them lead as much as they can: undressing, climbing in, soaping themselves
This is the most important routine to keep consistent — it signals sleep to the nervous system
daily_moments.what_this_builds: The bedtime routine as anchor, self-narration of sequence, internalized structure, the brain's wind-down signal

daily_moments.say_this: We're almost done with play time. In two minutes, it's time to clean up for dinner. I'll tell you when. Okay — it's time. First, blocks in the box. Then wash hands. Then sit at the table. You know the order. Blocks in the box — can you do it? You're doing it. Thank you. Next step — wash hands. You're moving through the routine. You know exactly what to do.
do_this: Give a warning before every transition: "In two minutes..." or "After one more..."
Name the sequence explicitly: "First ___, then ___, then ___"
Make transitions predictable — the same transitions happen at the same times
Praise their knowledge of the routine: "You knew what came next without me telling you"
If they resist, hold the structure: "I know you want to keep playing. This is what we do now."
daily_moments.what_this_builds: Transition tolerance, sequence internalization, self-directed movement through routine, the habit of finishing before starting

daily_moments.say_this: You're in pajamas, teeth are brushed, now it's book time. This is my favorite part of the routine. You pick one book. One book, then lights out. Which one tonight? Good choice. Let's read. [After reading:] Okay, book is done. What comes now? Sleep time. Lights go down. I love you. Same thing tomorrow — wake up, and our day starts all over again. Good night.
do_this: Identical sequence every night — no variation, no negotiation
One book (or two if that is your established routine — but always the same number)
Same closing words every night: your family's good-night phrase
If they try to extend: "One book. That's our routine. We can read more tomorrow."
Reference tomorrow: "When you wake up, we start our day again" — the rhythm is circular
daily_moments.what_this_builds: The non-negotiable bedtime sequence, closure, anticipation of tomorrow's rhythm, trust that the pattern holds

reflection_questions: Parts of the routine they anticipated without prompting
The daily "job" they did consistently
The transition that went smoothest
A moment they resisted the routine and how it resolved
[ ] Screens stayed under _____ min/day

title: Week 6: Sensory & Environmental Awareness
parent_frame: This week, go outside longer, look closer, and name the world together.
why_this_matters: Perpetual Sovereignty is built on direct engagement with physical reality. Science begins with observation. Land care begins with soil. Geography begins with the ground under your feet. All of these assume a child who notices the world — who looks at things, touches things, smells things, asks "what's that?" and "why?" A child whose early years are spent primarily indoors, primarily on screens, primarily in controlled environments arrives at Phase 1 with an attention deficit no curriculum can quickly fix. The world is the first and best classroom. Get them in it.
focus_items: Extend outdoor time — hours if possible, not minutes
Name everything in the natural world: trees, birds, clouds, insects, weather
Explore cause and effect: "What happens when you drop the rock in water?"
Experience weather as daily reality — rain, wind, cold, heat
Touch textures: bark, grass, mud, sand, stone, water, leaves
Begin seasonal awareness: "The leaves are changing. It's getting warmer."
keep_doing: Daily outdoor time · Consistent sleep & meal routines · Let them try before helping · Read aloud 2-3x daily · Name emotions in real time · "Please" and "thank you" modeled constantly
what_you_need: Appropriate outdoor clothing (boots, rain gear if you have it). A magnifying glass (optional but wonderful). A bucket or bag for collecting treasures. Willingness to get dirty.

daily_moments.say_this: Good morning! Let's look out the window. What do you see? Is it sunny or cloudy? It looks cloudy today. Feel the window — is it cold? The glass is cold because it's cool outside. We need a jacket today. Can you feel your shirt? It's soft — that's cotton. Now your jacket — feel that. It's different. It's smoother. We're going to be outside a lot today. Let's get ready for the weather.
do_this: Start the day by checking weather through the window or door
Name what they see and feel: sunny, cloudy, rainy, cold, warm, windy
Dress for the weather together — name why you are choosing each item
Touch clothing textures and compare: "This is soft. This is rough. This is smooth."
Make weather observation a daily habit, not just a Sensory Week habit
daily_moments.what_this_builds: Daily weather awareness, texture vocabulary, dressing with intention, connection between environment and preparation

daily_moments.say_this: Let's change your diaper. Feel this wipe — it's cold, isn't it? Cold and wet. Now feel the dry diaper — that's warm and dry. Cold and warm. Wet and dry. What do you hear right now? I hear the fan. And I hear birds outside. Can you hear them? Listen. What else? The clock is ticking. Tick, tick, tick. You notice so many things when you listen.
do_this: Use diaper change as a sensory-awareness moment: temperature, texture, sound
Ask "what do you hear?" and wait — name what they might be hearing
Touch different textures within reach: the changing pad, their clothes, the wipe
Contrast pairs: warm/cold, wet/dry, soft/rough, loud/quiet
Encourage stillness and listening — even briefly — during this routine
daily_moments.what_this_builds: Sensory vocabulary, auditory attention, awareness of texture contrasts, noticing the environment even during routine moments

daily_moments.say_this: Let's explore our food today. Touch the banana — what does it feel like? Smooth and soft. Now touch the cracker. That's rough and hard. Smell the orange — take a big sniff. What does it smell like? Sweet! Now taste it. Is it sweet or sour? Sweet and a little sour. What color is it? Orange! The fruit is called 'orange' and it IS orange. Listen when you bite the cracker — crunch! That's a loud food. The banana is quiet.
do_this: Engage all five senses with food: look, touch, smell, listen, taste
Name textures: smooth, bumpy, crunchy, soft, squishy, sticky
Name temperatures: warm soup, cold milk, room-temperature bread
Compare: "This apple is hard. This banana is soft."
Name smells: "Smell the cinnamon. Smell the bread."
Let them explore food with their hands — the mess is sensory learning
daily_moments.what_this_builds: Multi-sensory awareness, food vocabulary beyond taste, observation before consumption, descriptive language for physical properties

daily_moments.say_this: Let's go explore! Feel the ground — what are we walking on? Grass! Grass is soft and cool. Now we're on the sidewalk — that's hard and warm from the sun. Look up — what do you see in the sky? Clouds! Big white clouds. Are they moving? The wind is pushing them. Feel the wind on your face. Can you hear it? What else do you hear? Birds singing. Let's be quiet and just listen for a minute.
do_this: Walk slowly — this is exploration, not exercise
Touch everything safe: bark, leaves, rocks, soil, water, flowers, grass
Name textures and temperatures as they touch: "That bark is rough. That rock is smooth."
Listen together: stop, be quiet, identify sounds (birds, wind, cars, water, insects)
Smell: flowers, dirt after rain, cut grass, pine needles
Look closely: at bugs, at leaf patterns, at cracks in the sidewalk, at clouds
Let them collect natural objects: rocks, sticks, leaves, acorns
Name animals and plants you see: "That's a robin. See the red on its belly?"
daily_moments.what_this_builds: Deep environmental awareness, nature vocabulary, observation habits, multi-sensory engagement with the physical world, wonder

daily_moments.say_this: Let's play with water today. Feel it — is it warm or cold? Cold! Watch what happens when I pour it. See how it flows? Water always goes down. It doesn't go up. Now you pour. What happens when you put the rock in the water? Splash! And the rock sank to the bottom. It's heavy. Now try the leaf. It floats! The leaf is light. Heavy things sink. Light things float.
do_this: Set up sensory play: water, sand, playdough, mud, dried beans, rice
Explore cause and effect: "What happens when...?" Then let them try.
Sink/float experiments with household objects
Mix materials: water + dirt = mud. Dry sand vs. wet sand.
Temperature play: ice cubes melting, warm water vs. cold water
Let them get messy — contain the mess if needed (outside, in a bin) but do not prevent it
daily_moments.what_this_builds: Cause-and-effect understanding, physical properties vocabulary, experimentation habits, sensory tolerance, early scientific thinking

daily_moments.say_this: Bath time is full of science! Feel the water — warm. Now let's add cold water. Feel the difference? It's getting cooler. Watch the soap — I'm putting a drop in. Look at the bubbles! Pop one. What happened? It disappeared. Where did it go? Can you make more bubbles? Blow on the water. Now squeeze the sponge — feel all the water come out. It was full of water. Now it's dry.
do_this: Treat bath time as water exploration: pouring, temperature mixing, bubbles
Experiment: what floats, what sinks? Test different toys and objects
Cause and effect: squeezing sponges, popping bubbles, pouring from heights
Temperature awareness: "This side is warmer. This side is cooler."
Notice and name: water sounds (splash, drip, pour), water movement (swirl, flow, drip)
daily_moments.what_this_builds: Water physics intuition, temperature awareness, cause-and-effect through play, sensory vocabulary in a safe exploration environment

daily_moments.say_this: Time to clean up our nature treasures. Let's look at what we found today. This rock is smooth and gray. This leaf is yellow and crunchy. Feel them — which one is heavier? The rock! Let's put them in your nature collection. What was your favorite thing from outside today? The mud? You liked squishing the mud. I liked finding the ladybug. We noticed so many things.
do_this: Use cleanup as a moment to revisit what they explored
Keep a small nature collection: a box or basket for treasures from outside
Sort natural objects by texture, size, or color
Ask what they remember from sensory exploration: "What did you touch/hear/see?"
Transition language: "We're done exploring outside. Now we're going inside."
daily_moments.what_this_builds: Reflection on sensory experience, sorting natural objects, building observation memory, transition from exploration to next activity

daily_moments.say_this: Let's read a book about animals tonight. Look at this bear. Bears have fur — fur is soft and thick. Where do bears live? In the forest. We've been to the forest. Remember the tall trees? What did the trees feel like? Rough bark. This book shows nighttime. What happens at night? It gets dark. The moon comes out. Can you see the moon from your window? Let's look. There it is. Good night, moon. Good night, trees. Good night, world.
do_this: Choose books about nature, animals, weather, or seasons
Connect book content to real sensory experiences: "We saw a bird like that today"
Before sleep, do a sensory review: "What did you see/hear/touch/smell today?"
Look out the window at night: moon, stars, darkness, quiet
End with a calm sensory moment: the feel of the blanket, the sound of quiet, the smell of their stuffed animal
daily_moments.what_this_builds: Connecting book knowledge to real-world sensory experience, nighttime environmental awareness, reflection on the day's observations

reflection_questions: Something in nature they were fascinated by this week
A texture, smell, or sound they named on their own
How long they stayed engaged during outdoor exploration
A cause-and-effect discovery they made
[ ] Screens stayed under _____ min/day

title: Week 7: Independence & Practical Life
parent_frame: This week, let them do it themselves — even when it's messy and slow.
why_this_matters: The word "sovereignty" means self-rule. It begins here — not at age 14 when the student writes a rule of life, but at age 2 when the child pulls on their own shoes. Every act of genuine independence — choosing which shirt to wear, pouring their own water, carrying their plate to the sink — builds the identity of a person who does things for themselves. Every act of unnecessary dependence — being dressed when they could dress themselves, being carried when they could walk, having choices eliminated because it is faster — erodes that identity. This week, resist the urge to do it for them. Let them be slow. Let them be messy. Let them be capable.
focus_items: Let them dress themselves — accept backwards clothes as victories
Give them real household tasks: wiping surfaces, watering plants, sweeping
Offer limited choices: two options, both acceptable
Allow natural consequences: throw the cup, clean up the water
Practice self-feeding with utensils and open cups
Toileting awareness (by readiness, not by calendar)
keep_doing: Daily outdoor time · Consistent sleep & meal routines · Let them try before helping · Read aloud 2-3x daily · Name emotions in real time · "Please" and "thank you" modeled constantly
what_you_need: A step stool. A child-sized broom or dustpan. A small pitcher. A damp cloth for wiping. A small watering can. Patience.

daily_moments.say_this: Good morning! Time to get dressed. Do you want the blue shirt or the green shirt? You chose green — good choice. Now you put it on. Can you do it? Pull it over your head. Arms through. You're doing it! Is it backwards? That's okay — you put it on by yourself. That's what matters. Now pants. You know how. Step in. Pull up. You got dressed all by yourself today.
do_this: Lay out two choices. Let them pick. Do not override their choice.
Let them dress themselves completely before offering any help
If it is backwards or inside out, leave it. The independence matters more than the appearance.
If they get stuck, talk them through it before touching: "Pull the hole wider. Now push your arm in."
Only help after they ask or show real frustration — not mild frustration
Time does not matter. Being on time matters less than building this skill.
daily_moments.what_this_builds: Self-dressing, decision-making, problem-solving through physical challenge, the identity of "I can do this myself"

daily_moments.say_this: Diaper time. Can you pull your pants down? Pull them down — you're doing it. Now can you lie down? Good. I'll change you, but you help. Can you hold the clean diaper for me? Thank you. Now pull your pants back up. All the way up. You did most of it yourself. Do you feel wet or dry? Dry is comfortable, isn't it? Someday you'll use the potty when you're ready. No rush.
do_this: Let them pull their own pants down and up
Hand them the clean diaper to hold — they are participating, not being handled
Ask about wet/dry awareness: "Did you feel that? Wet or dry?"
If they show interest in the toilet, let them sit on it (clothed or not) — no pressure
Do not start potty training because of a calendar. Watch for readiness signs: awareness of wet/dry, interest in the toilet, staying dry for longer stretches
Treat their body with respect: narrate what you are doing, pause before acting
daily_moments.what_this_builds: Self-care participation, body awareness, toileting readiness (not training), the habit of doing for themselves what they can

daily_moments.say_this: Can you carry your plate to the table? Hold it with two hands. Careful — you did it! Now sit down. Here's your fork. Can you poke the chicken? You got it. Now try pouring your own water. Hold the pitcher with two hands. Tip it slowly. You're pouring! A little spilled — that's okay. Here's a cloth. Can you wipe it up? You poured your own water AND cleaned up the spill. That's real work.
do_this: Let them carry their own plate, cup, and utensils to the table
Provide a small pitcher with a small amount of water — let them pour
When they spill, hand them a cloth. They clean it. You do not clean it for them.
Self-feeding with utensils at every meal — expect mess, do not take over
Let them choose how much to eat. "More?" not "Eat more."
After the meal: they carry their plate to the counter or sink. This is their job, every meal.
daily_moments.what_this_builds: Self-feeding mastery, pouring independence, taking responsibility for spills, contribution to the meal routine, ownership of their place at the table

daily_moments.say_this: Can you put your shoes on? You try first. Push your foot in. Now the other one. You put your own shoes on! Can you open the door? Pull the handle. You opened it! Let's walk — can you carry the watering can to the garden? It's heavy. You're carrying it. Now pour the water on the plant. You're watering the garden. This is a real job and you're doing it.
do_this: Shoes on by themselves: accept them on the wrong feet (or help correct after they try)
Let them open doors, carry things, lead the walk
Give real outdoor tasks: watering plants, pulling weeds, carrying sticks to a pile
Let them choose the path: "Which way do you want to go?"
If they fall or stumble, wait before reacting. Many falls do not need adult intervention.
Let them problem-solve: stuck on a rock? climbing over a log? Give them time before you help.
daily_moments.what_this_builds: Outdoor independence, physical problem-solving, real contribution to household tasks, self-navigation, resilience after falls

daily_moments.say_this: What do you want to play with? You choose. The blocks? Okay. You get them out. Can you carry the box? Open it yourself. Now build whatever you want. I'm right here, but this is your project. You decide what to make. Uh oh — it fell. What do you want to do? Try again? Okay. You're figuring it out. You don't need me to do it. You've got this.
do_this: Let them choose the activity. Do not suggest or direct.
Let them get the materials out themselves: open containers, carry boxes, set up
Sit back. Narrate if they want company, but do not build for them
When they struggle, wait. Count to 30 in your head before helping.
If they ask for help, help the minimum amount: "What if you tried turning it?"
Let them play independently for stretches — do not fill every silence
daily_moments.what_this_builds: Self-directed play, independent problem-solving, frustration tolerance, the experience of "I figured it out myself"

daily_moments.say_this: Can you undress yourself for the bath? Pull your shirt off. Now your pants. You're doing it! Can you climb in? Hold the side. One leg over. You got in by yourself! Here's the soap. Can you wash your arms? Rub the soap — up and down. Now your legs. You're washing yourself. Can you wash your belly? You're taking your own bath. I'm just here to help with the hard parts.
do_this: Let them undress for the bath (as much as they can)
Let them climb in themselves (spot them, but let them do it)
Hand them the soap and washcloth — let them wash their own body
You do hair and any spots they miss, but they do the accessible parts
Let them squeeze out the washcloth, pour water to rinse
Getting out: let them climb out (with spotting), grab their own towel, try to dry themselves
daily_moments.what_this_builds: Self-care independence, body ownership, the routine of taking care of their own body, physical confidence in the bath

daily_moments.say_this: Play time is done. You know what to do — clean up before we start the next thing. Put the blocks in the box. You do it. I'll wait right here. You're putting them away — one at a time. It's slow, but you're doing it yourself. Every single block. All done? Let me see — the floor is clear. You cleaned your own space. That's your job, and you did it. What's next?
do_this: They clean up. Not you. You can help, but they do the majority.
Wait. Do not take over because it is slow.
If they resist, hold the expectation: "The next thing starts when this is clean."
Let them wipe their own high chair tray, push in their chair, put shoes by the door
Every transition is an opportunity for them to manage their own space
Praise the work, not the speed: "You cleaned up all by yourself."
daily_moments.what_this_builds: Ownership of their space, task completion, responsibility for their mess, independence through contribution

daily_moments.say_this: Let's get ready for bed. You know the routine. What's first? Pajamas — can you put them on? You try. Good — you got the pants on. Now the shirt. Pull it down. You're dressed for bed! Now teeth — hold the toothbrush. You brush first, then I'll finish. Good job. Now pick a book. You choose. Let's read together. [After reading:] You got ready for bed almost all by yourself tonight. You're growing up. Good night.
do_this: Let them lead the bedtime routine with minimal prompting
Pajamas: they do as much as they can. You finish what they cannot.
Toothbrushing: they brush first (it will be incomplete). You finish.
Book: they choose, they hold it if they want, they turn pages
Affirm their independence at the end of the day: "You did so much by yourself today"
The goal is not perfection — it is the pattern of trying first
daily_moments.what_this_builds: End-of-day independence review, the bedtime routine as self-directed practice, the identity of "I am someone who does things for myself"

reflection_questions: Something they did completely by themselves this week
A task where they surprised you with what they could do
Where they needed the most help still
A natural consequence they experienced and what they learned
[ ] Screens stayed under _____ min/day

title: Week 1: Language Acquisition
parent_frame: This week, listen to how your child tells stories — and help them tell better ones.
why_this_matters: Language is the tool that unlocks the entire Perpetual Sovereignty framework. Narration — the ability to listen to something and tell it back in your own words — is the primary assessment method across every pillar, every tier, every domain. A child who arrives at Phase 1 able to retell a story, describe an experience, follow three-step instructions, and hold a real conversation has the single most important capability the framework requires. This week, you are building that capability deliberately.
focus_items: Practice narration: after every read-aloud, ask your child to tell the story back
Give three-step instructions and let your child complete them without repeating
Have extended conversations — ask "why do you think...?" and "what would you do if...?"
Use precise vocabulary: "generous" not "nice," "exhausted" not "tired," "enormous" not "big"
Ask your child to describe their day to someone who was not there (the other parent, a grandparent, a stuffed animal)
Expect "please," "thank you," "excuse me," and "no thank you" without prompting
keep_doing: Daily outdoor time · Consistent routines with decreasing prompting · Read aloud 2-3x daily · Let them do everything they can for themselves · "Please," "thank you," "excuse me" expected without prompting · Chore ownership · Cleanup before transitions
what_you_need: Picture books with real stories (not just labeling books — stories with characters, problems, and resolutions), crayons and paper, a small pitcher for pouring practice, counting objects (buttons, stones, blocks)

daily_moments.say_this: Good morning. What do you think the weather is like today? ... Okay, if it's chilly, what should you wear? ... That's right — you'll need long sleeves. Go to your drawer, pick a shirt with long sleeves, pick pants, and bring them to your bed. I'll wait right here.
do_this: Let your child check the weather (look out the window) and reason about what to wear
Give the three-step instruction (get shirt, get pants, bring them to bed) and do not repeat it
If they forget a step, ask "What else did I say?" before repeating
Let them dress fully on their own — only help with difficult fasteners if asked
daily_moments.what_this_builds: Sequential processing, reasoning from evidence, independence in self-care

daily_moments.say_this: Before we eat, I want to hear about your dream last night. Did you dream anything? ... Tell me about it — what happened first? ... Then what? ... How did it end? ... That is a fascinating dream. Now, what would you like for breakfast — oatmeal or eggs? ... Okay, what do you need to do to get ready? ... Right — get your bowl, get a spoon, and sit down.
do_this: Ask your child to narrate something — a dream, something they remember from yesterday, a story from a book
Listen fully. Do not interrupt or correct. When they finish, ask one follow-up question
Let them get their own place setting (bowl, spoon, cup) and pour their own water from a small pitcher
Use precise food vocabulary: "This oatmeal has cinnamon — smell it. It's a warm, spicy smell."
daily_moments.what_this_builds: Narrative sequencing, descriptive vocabulary, self-service at meals

daily_moments.say_this: Let's be word collectors today. Every time we see something interesting, I want you to describe it to me using three words. Ready? Look at that tree — I would say 'enormous, rough, green.' Your turn. What do you see? ... Describe it with three words. ... Those are excellent words. Now tell me — why do you think that bird is hopping instead of flying?
do_this: Play "word collector" — challenge your child to describe things with specific adjectives
When your child uses a common word, offer a more precise one: "You said it's big — it really is. Another word for that is 'massive.'"
Ask open-ended "why do you think...?" questions about what you observe
After your outing, ask your child to tell someone else about it: "Tell Dad three things we saw on our walk"
daily_moments.what_this_builds: Precise vocabulary, observation-to-language connection, narrating experiences to others

daily_moments.say_this: What are you building? ... Tell me about it. Who lives there? ... What happens in this story? ... Oh, the dragon knocked it down? That sounds like a problem. What is the knight going to do about it? ... I like that solution. Tell me the whole story from the beginning — I want to hear it all.
do_this: During imaginative play, ask your child to narrate what is happening
Ask them to retell the "story" of their play from the beginning — this is narration practice in a low-pressure form
Introduce new vocabulary through play: "The castle has a drawbridge — that's the bridge that goes up and down over the moat"
Read a story aloud and then ask: "Now you tell it to me." Accept whatever they give. This is the beginning of formal narration
daily_moments.what_this_builds: Narrative structure (beginning, middle, end), story retelling, expressive language

daily_moments.say_this: It's time for your job. What is your chore today? ... That's right — setting the napkins. How many people are eating tonight? ... Four. So how many napkins do you need? ... Right, four. Go get them, fold them, and put one at each place. When you're finished, come tell me you're done.
do_this: Ask your child to state their chore verbally before doing it — this practices sequencing out loud
Let them report completion: "I'm done" is the beginning of accountability language
If they do it incompletely, ask: "Is it done, or is there more?" Let them self-assess
Use the chore as vocabulary practice: "You're folding the napkins in half — that's a rectangle shape now"
daily_moments.what_this_builds: Task sequencing, self-assessment, reporting completion, numeracy through real tasks

daily_moments.say_this: Tell me the three best things that happened today. ... Why was that your favorite? ... Now tell me one thing that was hard today. ... What made it hard? ... What did you do about it? ... That was a good choice. Now — can you wash your hair by yourself tonight? I'll watch, and you tell me each step as you do it.
do_this: Use bath time for daily narration practice — "Tell me about your day"
Ask follow-up questions that push for detail: "What happened next?" "How did that make you feel?"
Let your child narrate their own bath routine as they do it: "First I get my hair wet, then I put on shampoo..."
Introduce reflection language: "What was hard today?" builds the self-examination that IC1.1-A1 formalizes in its weekly review
daily_moments.what_this_builds: Reflective narration, emotional vocabulary, self-care independence, daily review habit

daily_moments.say_this: Tonight we're reading [title]. Before I start, look at the cover. What do you think this story is about? ... Let's find out. [Read the story.] ... Okay, now it's your turn. Tell me that story. What happened at the beginning? ... Then what? ... How did it end? ... You remembered the important parts. Was there a part you liked best?
do_this: Read a real story — with characters, a problem, and a resolution. Not a labeling book
After reading, ask your child to narrate it back. Do not prompt with questions unless they are stuck
If they give a very short retelling, ask one question: "What else happened?" Then accept what they give
Over weeks, narrations will naturally get longer and more detailed — this is the trend to watch
daily_moments.what_this_builds: Listening comprehension, narrative retelling, story structure awareness, the narration habit

daily_moments.say_this: We're about to switch to bath time. What do you need to do first? ... That's right — clean up. Tell me your plan: what goes where? ... Good. The blocks go in the bin, the books go on the shelf, and the crayons go in the cup. Go. I'll set a timer — can you beat three minutes?
do_this: Ask your child to verbalize the cleanup plan before starting — "What goes where?"
Let them direct the process. You are building their ability to sequence and instruct
After cleanup, ask: "Is it done? Look around — did you miss anything?"
Use cleanup transitions as a chance for your child to practice giving instructions: "Tell me where this goes"
daily_moments.what_this_builds: Planning language, self-directed task completion, transition management

reflection_questions: [ ] Screens stayed under _____ min/day

title: Week 2: Motor Development
parent_frame: This week, watch your child's hands and body — the strength, the coordination, the control. Everything from writing to building depends on what you see developing now.
why_this_matters: A child who is confident in their body is confident in the world. Motor competence is not just about sports or physical fitness — it is the foundation of writing (you cannot form letters without hand strength and coordination), practical skills (you cannot use tools without fine motor control), and even cognitive development (the child who explores space physically is building spatial reasoning). In Phase 1, your child will need to sit engaged for 10-15 minutes, hold a pencil with a functional grip, and complete physical tasks from start to finish. This week builds the body that makes all of that possible.
focus_items: Practice drawing with purpose — not just scribbling, but attempting to draw things (people, houses, animals)
Cutting practice with child-safe scissors — following a line, roughly
Throwing and catching with a large, soft ball
Balance activities: walking along a curb, standing on one foot, balancing on a beam
Dressing fully independently — only help with laces or difficult fasteners when asked
Observe pencil grip — gently encourage movement toward a tripod grip (thumb, index, middle finger)
keep_doing: Daily outdoor time · Consistent routines with decreasing prompting · Read aloud 2-3x daily · Let them do everything they can for themselves · "Please," "thank you," "excuse me" expected without prompting · Chore ownership · Cleanup before transitions
what_you_need: Child-safe scissors, paper with thick lines to cut along, crayons and markers, a large soft ball, drawing paper, balance beam or a low curb to practice on, play dough or clay for hand strengthening

daily_moments.say_this: Good morning. Time to get dressed. What's the plan for today — are we going outside or staying in? ... Okay, so pick something you can move in. Go get dressed — all the way. Shirt, pants, socks, shoes. I'll be in the kitchen. Come find me when you're completely ready.
do_this: Let your child dress completely independently — you should not be in the room
If they come out with something backwards, ask: "Does that feel right? Check in the mirror" — let them self-correct
Time how long it takes without pressuring. Over weeks, it should get faster naturally
Buttons, zippers, and Velcro should all be attempted independently. Help only when asked
daily_moments.what_this_builds: Fine motor (fasteners), sequencing, independence, self-assessment

daily_moments.say_this: Today I want you to help me make breakfast. Can you crack an egg? Here — tap it on the edge of the bowl, then pull it apart with your thumbs. ... Good try! It takes practice. Now stir — hold the spoon like this, and move it in circles. ... You're doing it. Can you spread the butter on your toast? Use the flat part of the knife and push it across.
do_this: Involve your child in real food preparation: cracking eggs, stirring batter, spreading butter, pouring from a small pitcher
Let them use a real (butter) knife for spreading — supervised, but real
Observe their grip on utensils: fork, spoon, knife. Are they using fingers, not a fist?
Let them pour their own drink from a small pitcher. Expect occasional spills — they are learning
daily_moments.what_this_builds: Hand strength, bilateral coordination, grip refinement, kitchen competence

daily_moments.say_this: Let's play catch. I'm going to throw the ball to you — watch it all the way into your hands. Ready? ... Nice try! You almost had it. Keep your eyes on the ball. ... Great catch! Now throw it back to me. Use your whole arm. ... Now let's try something hard — can you walk along this curb without stepping off? Arms out for balance. How far can you go?
do_this: Spend 10-15 minutes on throwing and catching practice. Use a large, soft ball and stand close
Practice balance: walking along a curb, a low wall, a beam, or a line on the ground
Let your child climb — trees, playground equipment, rocks. Manage risk, but do not eliminate it
Run, jump, hop on one foot. Physical confidence comes from doing, not from being careful
daily_moments.what_this_builds: Hand-eye coordination, balance, risk assessment, gross motor confidence

daily_moments.say_this: Let's draw today. I want you to draw our family. Think about it — who should be in the picture? ... Good. Now draw each person. Give them eyes, a mouth, arms, legs. Take your time — I want to see the details. ... Tell me about your picture. Who is that? What are they doing? ... Now let's practice cutting. See this line? Try to cut along it. Keep the scissors moving forward.
do_this: Drawing time: ask for representational drawing (people, houses, animals), not free scribbling
Observe pencil grip. If they are using a fist grip, gently show tripod grip: "Try holding it with these three fingers"
Cutting practice: draw thick curved and straight lines on paper and have your child cut along them
Play dough time: squeezing, rolling, pinching. This builds the hand strength that supports pencil grip
daily_moments.what_this_builds: Fine motor control, pencil grip development, representational thinking, bilateral coordination (scissors)

daily_moments.say_this: Your chore today is wiping down the table after lunch. Here's the cloth — get it damp, wring it out, and wipe the whole table. Not just the middle — the edges too. When you think it's done, run your hand across it. Does it feel clean? ... What about this spot? ... Good. Now wring out the cloth and hang it up. The whole job, start to finish.
do_this: Choose a chore that requires motor coordination: wiping a table, sweeping with a child-sized broom, watering plants with a can
Emphasize the complete task: setup, the work, and cleanup of tools afterward
Let them self-assess: "Is it done? Check it yourself"
Notice and name the motor skills involved: "You're wringing that cloth out well — that takes strong hands"
daily_moments.what_this_builds: Hand strength, bilateral coordination, task completion, self-assessment of quality

daily_moments.say_this: Can you do the whole bath yourself tonight? I'll sit right here, but you do everything. Wash your body — start at the top and work down. Tell me what you're washing as you go. ... Good. Now your hair — wet it, soap it, rinse it. ... What comes after the bath? ... Right — dry off, hang up the towel, put on pajamas. The whole thing.
do_this: Let your child manage the entire bath routine independently, with you present but not helping
Ask them to narrate what they are doing — this combines motor practice with language
Have them handle the towel, hang it up, and dress in pajamas without help
Observe: can they manage all the fine motor tasks (soap bottle, washcloth wringing, buttons on pajamas)?
daily_moments.what_this_builds: Self-care motor sequence, full-body coordination, independence, task completion

daily_moments.say_this: Before our book, let's do something with your hands. Hold up your fingers — can you touch each finger to your thumb? Thumb to pointer, thumb to middle, thumb to ring, thumb to pinky. Try it on both hands. ... Good. Now let's read. [Read story.] ... Tomorrow during drawing time, I want you to draw a picture of something from this story. Think about what you'd draw.
do_this: Do a brief fine motor warm-up before reading: finger touching, hand squeezes, wrist circles
Read a story that involves physical action — characters building, climbing, making things
After reading, ask your child to narrate back — keep the narration habit going every night
Preview tomorrow's drawing activity: "Think about what you'd draw from this story." This builds the planning habit
daily_moments.what_this_builds: Fine motor awareness, sustained attention during read-aloud, narration habit, planning

daily_moments.say_this: Time to clean up. Look at this room. What needs to go where? ... Tell me your plan. ... Good plan. Now do it — but this time, I want you to carry things with two hands when they're heavy and one hand when they're light. Pay attention to how your hands work.
do_this: Let your child direct the cleanup — they identify what goes where and execute the plan
Make it physical: carrying bins, stacking blocks, lifting books onto shelves
Notice and name their motor skills: "You stacked those carefully — that takes balance and coordination"
Expect complete cleanup: "Look around. Is everything where it belongs?"
daily_moments.what_this_builds: Spatial organization, motor planning, task completion, transition management

reflection_questions: [ ] Screens stayed under _____ min/day

title: Week 3: Numeracy Roots
parent_frame: This week, count everything — but go further. Ask 'how many?' and watch your child discover that the last number is the answer.
why_this_matters: A child who arrives at Phase 1 with strong numeracy roots — who has spent years counting real objects, comparing "more" and "less," recognizing patterns, and hearing mathematical language in daily life — will find formal numeracy natural. The Perpetual Sovereignty framework begins formal quantitative work in Phase 2 with Pillar 1.5 (Quantitative Sovereignty), but IC1.1-A1 already expects a child who can follow sequential steps and complete multi-part tasks. Numeracy roots are not about arithmetic. They are about the ability to see order, pattern, and quantity in the real world — and that ability starts here.
focus_items: Count with one-to-one correspondence: one number per object, finger pointing to each
Practice cardinality: after counting, ask "how many?" — the answer is the last number said
Simple addition and subtraction with real objects ("You have three grapes. I'll give you two more. How many now?")
Create patterns: red-blue-red-blue, clap-stomp-clap-stomp, AB, ABB, ABC
Expand shape and spatial vocabulary: rectangle, oval, diamond, "between," "behind," "above," "below"
Use measurement language: "Which is heavier?" "Who is taller?" "Is there enough?"
keep_doing: Daily outdoor time · Consistent routines with decreasing prompting · Read aloud 2-3x daily · Let them do everything they can for themselves · "Please," "thank you," "excuse me" expected without prompting · Chore ownership · Cleanup before transitions
what_you_need: Counting objects (buttons, stones, dried beans, blocks), a set of objects for sorting (mixed colors, shapes, or sizes), paper and crayons for pattern-drawing, a simple balance or kitchen scale, measuring cups, things to compare (sticks of different lengths, containers of different sizes)

daily_moments.say_this: Good morning. Let's count while you get dressed. How many pieces of clothing do you need to put on? Let's figure it out — underwear, that's one. Shirt, that's two. Pants, three. Socks — four and five. Shoes — six and seven. Seven things! Can you get all seven on by yourself? Count them as you go.
do_this: Turn getting dressed into a counting activity — how many items? How many buttons? How many steps?
Ask cardinality questions: "You put on seven things. How many are you wearing now?"
Introduce spatial language: "Your shirt tag goes behind your neck. Your socks go inside your shoes."
Let them dress independently while counting — combining motor independence with numeracy
daily_moments.what_this_builds: One-to-one correspondence, cardinality, spatial language, self-care independence

daily_moments.say_this: You're setting the table today. How many people are eating? ... Four. So how many plates do you need? ... Four, right — one for each person. Now how many forks? ... Good. Now let's count your strawberries. Point to each one as you count. ... How many? ... Six! If you eat two, how many will be left? ... Let's find out.
do_this: Table setting is a one-to-one correspondence activity: one plate per person, one fork per person, one napkin per person
Count food items at the plate. Ask "how many?" after counting — check for cardinality understanding
Do real subtraction: "You had six strawberries. You ate two. Count what's left." Then real addition: "Here are three more."
Use comparison: "Who has more blueberries? Who has less? How could we make it the same?"
daily_moments.what_this_builds: One-to-one correspondence, cardinality, addition/subtraction with real objects, comparison language

daily_moments.say_this: Let's go on a pattern walk. Look — the fence is post, space, post, space, post, space. That's an AB pattern. Can you find another pattern outside? ... Good eye! Now let's collect things. Find me five sticks — count them into my hand. ... Five! Now let's sort them — which is longest? Shortest? Can you put them in order from shortest to longest?
do_this: Look for patterns in the environment: fence posts, bricks, leaves, petals
Collect natural objects and count them with one-to-one correspondence
Sort collections: by size (longest to shortest), by color, by type
Measure and compare: "Which stick is longer? Let's put them next to each other. Which rock is heavier? Hold one in each hand."
daily_moments.what_this_builds: Pattern recognition, sorting and comparing, measurement language, observation skills

daily_moments.say_this: Let's make patterns with blocks. I'll start: red, blue, red, blue. What comes next? ... Right! Red. Now let's try a harder one: red, red, blue, red, red, blue. What comes next? ... Now you make one for me. I'll try to figure out the rule. ... Hmm, let me look. Green, yellow, yellow, green, yellow, yellow — is the next one green? ... Now let's count all the blocks. Line them up and point to each one.
do_this: Pattern creation: start with AB patterns, move to ABB and ABC. Have your child create patterns for you to extend
Build towers and count: "How many blocks tall is your tower? Let's count from the bottom."
Shape recognition: "What shape is this block? Can you find all the rectangles? How is a rectangle different from a square?"
Simple addition through play: "You have four blocks. I'm giving you three more. How many do you have now?"
daily_moments.what_this_builds: Pattern creation and extension, shape identification, counting with correspondence, mathematical conversation

daily_moments.say_this: We're going to water the plants today. There are five plants. Each one gets one cup of water — not two, not zero, one. Count as you go: one cup for this plant, one cup for that plant. ... How many have you watered? ... How many are left? ... Good. When you're done, how many cups of water did you use altogether?
do_this: Turn chores into counting and one-to-one correspondence activities
Watering plants: one cup per plant. Setting table: one of each item per person. Sorting laundry: count items by color
Ask "how many?" questions throughout: "How many plants did you water? How many are left?"
Introduce measurement language during chores: "Is this cup full or half-full? Pour until it reaches the line."
daily_moments.what_this_builds: One-to-one correspondence in real tasks, counting with purpose, measurement awareness, chore ownership

daily_moments.say_this: Let's do some water math. I'm going to pour water from this cup into that cup. Watch — is the big cup full? ... Not yet. How many small cups do you think it will take to fill the big cup? ... Let's count and find out. One... two... three. Three! You guessed two — that was close. Now you try. How many pours to fill this one?
do_this: Water play is measurement play: pouring between containers of different sizes
Estimation practice: "How many small cups will it take to fill the big one?" Guess first, then test
Counting body parts: "How many fingers? How many toes? How many on each hand? Are they the same?"
Temperature language: "Is the water warm or cool? Which is hotter — this side or that side?"
daily_moments.what_this_builds: Estimation, measurement through pouring, comparison, prediction and testing

daily_moments.say_this: Tonight let's read a story with numbers in it. [Read story.] ... How many [bears/pigs/kittens] were in that story? ... What happened when one more came? ... What happened when one left? ... Now you tell me the story back. ... Good. Before you go to sleep, let's count to twenty together. Ready? One, two, three...
do_this: Choose books that involve counting, patterns, or quantity (many picture books do naturally)
After reading, ask numeracy questions alongside narration: "How many characters? What happened when one left?"
Practice the counting sequence: count together to 20 or as high as your child can go reliably
End with narration: have your child retell the story. Keep this habit every night regardless of the week's focus
daily_moments.what_this_builds: Number sense through stories, counting sequence practice, narration habit, listening comprehension

daily_moments.say_this: Time to clean up. Let's count what needs to be put away. How many things are on the floor? ... Count them with me — point to each one. ... Eight things. Can you put them all away before I count to thirty? Ready? One, two, three...
do_this: Count the mess: "How many things need to be put away?" Count together with pointing
Sort before putting away: "Put all the books together, all the blocks together, all the animals together"
Race the count: you count slowly to 30 while they clean. This builds speed and urgency around cleanup
After cleanup: "How many things did you put away? Is the room done?"
daily_moments.what_this_builds: Counting with purpose, sorting by category, task completion, transition management
