title: Week 4: Social-Emotional Foundation
parent_frame: This week, pay attention to how your child handles frustration, disappointment, and waiting. This is where self-governance begins.
why_this_matters: The Inner Core of Perpetual Sovereignty — Character, Discipline, Resilience — is the operating system for everything else in the framework. IC1.1-A1, the very first packet, expects a child who can wait without falling apart, stop when told to stop, recover from disappointment, and begin to distinguish between wants and needs. None of these capabilities appear spontaneously. They are built through thousands of small moments where a parent names the emotion, validates it, holds the boundary, and then helps the child move forward. You are building the emotional architecture that self-governance requires.
focus_items: Extend frustration tolerance deliberately — "I know you want it now. We'll do it after lunch."
Introduce "want vs. need" language: "Do you need that, or do you want it?"
Practice recovery from disappointment — name it, validate it, move on: "What should we do now?"
Coach peer conflict instead of rescuing: "Tell them what you want. Use your words."
Practice "stop" compliance — when you say "stop," your child stops within seconds, reliably
Notice and name when your child uses emotion words spontaneously: "I heard you say you were frustrated. That's exactly the right word."
keep_doing: Daily outdoor time · Consistent routines with decreasing prompting · Read aloud 2-3x daily · Let them do everything they can for themselves · "Please," "thank you," "excuse me" expected without prompting · Chore ownership · Cleanup before transitions
what_you_need: No special materials. The curriculum this week is the emotional reality of daily life — the frustrations, the waits, the disappointments, and the recoveries that happen naturally. Books about emotions and characters who face hard situations are helpful for read-aloud time.

daily_moments.say_this: Good morning. I know sometimes you don't feel like getting up. That's okay — you don't have to feel like it. You just have to do it. How are you feeling this morning? ... Tired? I understand. What's the first thing you do? ... Right — bathroom, then get dressed. Even when you're tired, the routine stays the same. That's what makes it a routine.
do_this: Acknowledge how your child feels without letting it change the expectation
Model the principle: feelings are real, and the routine happens anyway. This is the core of IC1.1-A1
Ask "How are you feeling?" and accept the answer. Name it if they cannot: "You seem grumpy. That's okay. Let's get moving."
Do not rush them emotionally, but do hold the time expectation: "You have until I count to ten to start getting dressed"
daily_moments.what_this_builds: Emotional awareness, the separation of feelings from actions, routine compliance

daily_moments.say_this: We're having oatmeal this morning. I know you wanted pancakes. Are pancakes something you need, or something you want? ... That's right — you want them. You need breakfast, and oatmeal is breakfast. It's okay to be disappointed. You can say 'I'm disappointed.' Now — what topping do you want on your oatmeal? You get to choose that.
do_this: Use mealtime to practice want vs. need language. Food preferences are a natural arena: "You need food. You want a specific food."
When your child is disappointed about what's being served, name-validate-hold: "You're disappointed. I understand. This is what we're having."
Offer a choice within the boundary: they cannot choose the meal, but they can choose the topping or the cup
If your child uses an emotion word spontaneously ("I'm mad" or "I'm sad"), affirm it: "Thank you for telling me how you feel. That's exactly right."
daily_moments.what_this_builds: Want vs. need distinction, disappointment recovery, emotional vocabulary, choice within boundaries

daily_moments.say_this: Let's play freeze tag. When I say 'stop,' you freeze — no more moving. Ready? Run! ... STOP. ... Good — you stopped fast. That was about two seconds. Let's try again and see if you can stop even faster. ... STOP. ... One second! Even better. Now let's play red light, green light. Green light means go. Red light means stop immediately.
do_this: Play stop games: freeze tag, red light/green light, Simon Says. These are self-regulation training disguised as play
Time the response: "I said stop and you stopped in two seconds. Can you do it in one?"
Practice in real situations too: if your child is running toward something, call "stop" and expect a prompt response
After games, name what happened: "You stopped your whole body when I said stop. That takes self-control. That's a real skill."
daily_moments.what_this_builds: Impulse inhibition, response to authority, self-control through practice, physical self-regulation

daily_moments.say_this: You've been working on that puzzle for a while and it's hard. I can see you're getting frustrated. What do you do when something is hard? ... That's right — you can take a deep breath, and you can try a different piece. You don't throw it. You don't quit. You take a breath and try again. ... There! You got it. How do you feel now? ... Proud? You should be. You stuck with it.
do_this: When your child encounters frustration during play, do NOT rescue immediately. Wait. Count to ten silently
If they express frustration verbally ("This is hard!" or "I can't do it!"), validate and coach: "It is hard. Try again."
If they express frustration physically (throwing, hitting, yelling), name-redirect: "You're frustrated. You can say 'I'm frustrated.' You cannot throw the pieces."
After they work through frustration, name the skill: "You kept going even when it was hard. That is called perseverance."
daily_moments.what_this_builds: Frustration tolerance, emotional vocabulary, perseverance, the habit of continuing through difficulty

daily_moments.say_this: It's chore time. I know you'd rather keep playing. Do you need to keep playing, or do you want to keep playing? ... Right — you want to. But your chore needs to be done. What's your chore? ... Good. Go do it. When it's finished, you can go back to playing. The playing will be there when you get back.
do_this: Use chore time to practice delayed gratification: the fun activity waits until the responsibility is complete
Reinforce want vs. need: "You want to play. You need to do your chore. The chore comes first."
Do not hover. Let them do the chore independently. If they complain, acknowledge and hold: "I hear you. The chore still needs to be done."
When they finish, name what happened: "You did something you didn't feel like doing. That is self-discipline."
daily_moments.what_this_builds: Delayed gratification, want vs. need in action, self-discipline language, chore ownership

daily_moments.say_this: Tell me about your feelings today. What made you happy? ... What made you frustrated? ... What made you feel proud? ... Was there a time you had to wait for something? How did that go? ... Was there a time you were disappointed? What did you do? ... I like how you're thinking about your day. Knowing how you feel is a really important skill.
do_this: Use bath time as a nightly emotional check-in. Ask about specific emotions, not just "how was your day?"
Help your child build a feelings vocabulary beyond happy/sad/mad: frustrated, disappointed, proud, nervous, excited, grateful, embarrassed
When they describe a moment of difficulty, ask: "What did you do about it?" This builds the habit of linking emotion to action
If they cannot name an emotion, offer options: "Were you frustrated, or were you disappointed? Those are different feelings."
daily_moments.what_this_builds: Emotional vocabulary expansion, reflective self-awareness, emotion-to-action connection, daily review habit

daily_moments.say_this: Tonight's story has a character who faces something hard. Let's see what they do. [Read story.] ... What happened to [character]? How do you think they felt? ... What did they do about it? ... Was that a good choice? What would you have done? ... Now you tell me the story. What happened?
do_this: Choose stories where characters face emotional challenges: disappointment, fear, frustration, waiting
After reading, ask emotional comprehension questions: "How did the character feel? Why?"
Ask your child what they would do: "What would you have done?" This builds empathy and decision-making
Always end with narration: "Now tell me the story." The narration habit continues regardless of the week's focus
daily_moments.what_this_builds: Emotional comprehension, empathy, perspective-taking, narration habit, character reasoning

daily_moments.say_this: We're switching activities in five minutes. I'm telling you now so you can finish what you're doing. When I say 'time's up,' what happens? ... Right — you stop what you're doing, clean up, and move to the next thing. Even if you're not done. Even if you don't want to. Ready? ... Time's up. Stop and clean up.
do_this: Give a warning before transitions: "Five minutes." Then "Two minutes." Then "Time's up."
When time is up, expect compliance. Name-validate-hold if needed: "I know you want to keep playing. Time is up. Clean up now."
If your child transitions well, name it: "You stopped playing and started cleaning up without arguing. That is excellent self-control."
If they struggle, stay calm and present. Do not negotiate. Wait for compliance. "I'll wait right here. When you're ready to clean up, I'm here."
daily_moments.what_this_builds: Transition tolerance, response to authority, emotional regulation during change, cleanup habit

reflection_questions: [ ] Screens stayed under _____ min/day

title: Week 5: Routine & Habit Formation
parent_frame: This week, step back and watch — can your child move through the morning routine without you directing each step?
why_this_matters: IC1.1-A1 — the very first packet in Perpetual Sovereignty — is about habits, routines, and first self-control. It expects a child who can follow a daily routine without being prompted at each step. That capacity does not appear at age four because a parent decides it is time. It is built through years of living inside a predictable rhythm and gradually taking ownership of that rhythm. This week, you are deliberately building the exact capability that IC1.1-A1 formalizes. In Phase 1, your child will use a visual schedule, own chores, and move through their day with decreasing adult direction. The bridge to that starts now.
focus_items: Create or update a visual schedule (pictures showing the morning routine, posted on the wall)
Practice: your child looks at the visual schedule and tells you "what comes next" without you saying it
Morning routine with no more than 1-2 reminders (wake, bathroom, dress, breakfast, chore)
Chore ownership: one chore that is theirs — not "helping," but owning. They notice it needs doing and do it
Cleanup before every transition — expected, not optional
Begin tracking: how many reminders did you give today?
keep_doing: Daily outdoor time · Consistent routines with decreasing prompting · Read aloud 2-3x daily · Let them do everything they can for themselves · "Please," "thank you," "excuse me" expected without prompting · Chore ownership · Cleanup before transitions
what_you_need: Materials for a visual schedule: printed pictures or hand-drawn images of each routine step, posted at your child's eye level. Velcro dots or checkboxes so your child can mark each step as done. A consistent chore assignment. A consistent daily rhythm that does not change day to day.

daily_moments.say_this: Good morning. What's the first thing on your schedule? ... Go look at it. ... Right — bathroom first. What's after that? ... Brush teeth. Then? ... Get dressed. Then? ... Come to breakfast. You know the whole thing. Go do it — I'll be in the kitchen. Come find me when you've done every step.
do_this: Point your child to the visual schedule, not to your voice. They reference the schedule, not you
Do not follow them through the routine. Stay in another room. Let them manage it
When they come to you, ask: "Did you do every step? Let's check the schedule." Let them self-assess
Count your reminders. The goal is two or fewer. If you gave more, that is information — not failure. Adjust and try again tomorrow
daily_moments.what_this_builds: Self-directed routine, visual schedule use, self-assessment, independence

daily_moments.say_this: What comes after breakfast on your schedule? ... Right — chore time. But first, what do you do with your dishes? ... Take your plate and cup to the sink. Wipe your spot. Push in your chair. That's the mealtime routine — same every time. Do you need me to remind you, or do you remember? ... Good. Go do it.
do_this: Mealtime has its own mini-routine: sit down, eat, clear plate, wipe spot, push in chair
This routine should be the same at every meal. Repetition is the mechanism — the body learns the sequence
Ask your child to state the mealtime routine before doing it: "What are the steps after you finish eating?"
If they forget a step, point to the area (not the action): "Look at the table. Is it done?" Let them figure out what is missing
daily_moments.what_this_builds: Routine within routine, self-directed cleanup, habit formation through repetition

daily_moments.say_this: Before we go outside, what's the routine? ... Right — shoes on, check the weather, decide what you need. Do you need a jacket? You decide. ... Good choice. What do you always bring to the park? ... Your water bottle. Go get it. Now — what's the rule when we're outside? ... We come in when I say it's time, even if you're not ready. Even if you're having fun.
do_this: Going outside has a routine: shoes, weather check, gear decision, water bottle, go
Let your child own this sequence. Do not hand them their shoes or their water bottle
Establish and reinforce the return routine: "When I say it's time to go, we go. No arguments. You can say 'I'm disappointed,' but we still go."
After outdoor time, the return routine: shoes off in their spot, water bottle put away, wash hands
daily_moments.what_this_builds: Pre-activity routine, gear management, transition compliance, independence

daily_moments.say_this: You can choose what to play. But remember the rule — when you're done with one thing, you clean it up before you start the next thing. I'm not going to remind you. If I see you start something new without cleaning up, I'll ask you one question: 'What did you forget?' That's your signal. You know the routine.
do_this: This week, step back during play and watch: does your child clean up between activities without prompting?
If they switch without cleaning up, do not clean it up or lecture. Simply ask: "What did you forget?"
When they self-correct, name it: "You remembered on your own. That's the routine working."
If they play with one thing for an extended time (15-20 minutes of sustained engagement), notice that too: "You've been building for twenty minutes. That focus is impressive."
daily_moments.what_this_builds: Cleanup-before-transition habit, sustained engagement, self-monitoring, self-correction

daily_moments.say_this: This is your chore. Nobody else does it — it's yours. What is it? ... Setting napkins at dinner. When does it need to happen? ... Before dinner. I'm not going to remind you today. When it's almost dinnertime, you should notice and do it. If dinner comes and it's not done, we'll talk about what happened. Can you own this?
do_this: This week, do not remind your child about their chore. See if they notice and do it independently
If they forget, do not do it for them. Wait until the natural consequence is visible (dinner starts, no napkins set) and ask: "What happened with your chore?"
If they remember on their own, name it specifically: "You noticed it was almost dinner and you set the napkins without anyone telling you. That is ownership."
Discuss: "In Phase 1, you'll have chores that are all yours. You're already practicing that."
daily_moments.what_this_builds: Initiative, ownership, internal motivation, accountability

daily_moments.say_this: What's the bath routine? Tell me the steps before you start. ... Good — water on, get in, wash body, wash hair, rinse, water off, dry off, pajamas, brush teeth. You know every step. Now do it. I'll sit right here. If you need help, ask. But try everything yourself first.
do_this: Ask your child to state the bath routine before starting — verbalizing the sequence reinforces it
Let them manage the entire routine independently. Be present but not directing
If they skip a step, wait and see if they self-correct. If not, ask: "What step comes after washing your body?"
After: "You did the whole bath routine by yourself. How many reminders did you need? ... Zero? That's ready for Phase 1."
daily_moments.what_this_builds: Full self-care routine, self-direction, sequence memory, independence

daily_moments.say_this: What's the bedtime routine? Walk me through it. ... Book, then prayer or quiet time, then lights out. Same every night. Before we read — tell me how today went. What did you do well today? ... What was hard? ... What do you want to do better tomorrow? ... Those are good answers. Now let's read.
do_this: Begin each bedtime with a brief review of the day — this is the earliest form of IC1.1-A1 Practice 5 (Weekly Review)
Ask three questions: What went well? What was hard? What will you do better tomorrow?
Do not judge the answers. Listen and affirm: "I like that you're thinking about it."
Read the story, ask for narration, then follow the bedtime routine in exact order. Every night. Same order.
daily_moments.what_this_builds: Self-assessment, reflective habit, routine consistency, narration practice

daily_moments.say_this: We're switching in two minutes. What do you need to do before we switch? ... Right — clean up what you're using. You've got two minutes. I'm not going to remind you again — you know the routine. When I say 'time,' everything should be put away. Ready? Go.
do_this: Give one warning and one time call. No repeated reminders in between
If they transition cleanly, name it: "You heard the time call and cleaned up without a single reminder. That's the routine working."
If they do not transition cleanly, hold calmly: "The next activity doesn't start until this one is cleaned up. I'll wait."
Track transition quality this week: how many transitions needed more than one reminder?
daily_moments.what_this_builds: Transition management, cleanup habit, response to time cues, self-regulation

reflection_questions: [ ] Screens stayed under _____ min/day

title: Week 6: Sensory & Environmental Awareness
parent_frame: This week, go outside with new eyes — draw what you see, track the weather, and watch the world like a scientist.
why_this_matters: Perpetual Sovereignty is built on direct engagement with physical reality. Science begins with observation. Land care begins with soil. Survival begins with awareness of your environment. All of these assume a child who notices the world — who looks at things closely, asks "what's that?" and "why?", and can describe what they see to someone else. In Phase 1, your child will begin nature journaling, scientific observation, and geographical awareness. This week, you are building the habit of attention that makes all of that possible. A child who has spent years outdoors, observing, naming, and wondering arrives at Phase 1 ready. A child who has not will struggle with every domain that starts with "look."
focus_items: Nature journaling begins: bring paper and crayons outside and draw what you observe (a leaf, a rock, a bug, a cloud)
Daily weather tracking: "What's the weather today?" becomes a ritual question every morning
Extended outdoor time — not a 15-minute walk, but an hour or more if possible
Gardening: plant something, water it, watch it over time
Animal observation: watch birds, bugs, squirrels. Ask questions: "What is it doing? Where is it going?"
Name the natural world with precision: "That's an oak tree, not just a tree." "That bird is a cardinal — see the red?"
keep_doing: Daily outdoor time · Consistent routines with decreasing prompting · Read aloud 2-3x daily · Let them do everything they can for themselves · "Please," "thank you," "excuse me" expected without prompting · Chore ownership · Cleanup before transitions
what_you_need: Paper and crayons for outdoor drawing, a magnifying glass (optional but wonderful), a simple weather chart posted where your child can reach it, seeds and a small pot or garden space, a watering can, binoculars (optional), a nature bag or basket for collecting

daily_moments.say_this: Good morning. Before you get dressed, go look out the window. What's the weather? ... Cloudy and cool. So what season are we in? ... That's right. Go update the weather chart — draw a cloud on today's square. Now, what should you wear for cloudy and cool weather? ... Good thinking. Go get dressed.
do_this: Make weather observation the first thing every morning. Your child checks, reports, and records
Post a simple weather chart on the wall: a grid with days of the week where your child draws a sun, cloud, rain, or snow each morning
Connect weather to reasoning: "It's cloudy — will you need a jacket?" The child makes the decision
Over weeks, look at the weather chart together: "We had three rainy days this week. Last week we had one. What do you notice?"
daily_moments.what_this_builds: Observation habit, weather awareness, recording data, reasoning from evidence

daily_moments.say_this: Let's talk about where our food comes from. These eggs — where do they come from? ... Chickens, right. And these strawberries — they grew on a plant. Have you ever seen a strawberry plant? It grows close to the ground. The berries start green, then turn white, then red. When they're red, they're ready. What about milk — where does that come from?
do_this: Use mealtime to trace food to its source: where does it grow? What does the plant look like? What animal does it come from?
Let your child help prepare food and observe: "Watch what happens when we crack this egg. What's inside? The yellow part is the yolk."
Introduce sensory vocabulary through food: "This orange is tart — that means a little sour. This apple is crisp. Smell the basil — that's an herb."
Connect to gardening: "We could grow basil. Would you like to try?"
daily_moments.what_this_builds: Source awareness, sensory vocabulary, agricultural curiosity, observation through food

daily_moments.say_this: Today we're going to be scientists. Scientists look very carefully at things, and then they draw what they see. Find one thing you want to study — a leaf, a flower, a bug, a rock. Look at it closely. What do you notice? ... What color is it? What shape? Is it smooth or rough? Now draw it. Try to draw exactly what you see — the shape, the lines, the details.
do_this: Nature journaling: sit outside with paper and crayons. Your child picks one object and draws it while looking at it
The drawing does not need to be "good." It needs to be observational — an attempt to capture what they see
Ask observation questions while they draw: "How many petals does that flower have? What shape are the leaves? Is one side different from the other?"
Collect things in a nature bag to bring home, examine further, or draw again later
If there is an insect or animal to watch, stop and observe: "What is the ant doing? Where is it going? Watch for one minute."
daily_moments.what_this_builds: Observational drawing, attention to detail, patience in observation, nature vocabulary, scientific habit of mind

daily_moments.say_this: Let's do a sensory experiment. Close your eyes. I'm going to put three things in your hands, one at a time. Tell me what you notice — is it hard or soft? Heavy or light? Smooth or rough? Cold or warm? ... Now open your eyes. Were you right? ... Let's try something else. Close your eyes and listen. What sounds do you hear? How many different sounds? Tell me each one.
do_this: Sensory awareness activities: texture sorting (smooth vs. rough), weight comparison (heavy vs. light), temperature (warm water vs. cool water)
Blindfold games: identify objects by touch alone, identify sounds with eyes closed
Build with natural materials: sticks, stones, leaves, mud. What can you make from what you find outside?
Plant a seed today if you have not already: place it in soil, water it, put it in sunlight. "We'll check it every day and see what happens."
daily_moments.what_this_builds: Sensory discrimination, descriptive language, cause-and-effect awareness, patience (gardening)

daily_moments.say_this: Your plant needs water. Go check the soil first — push your finger in. Does it feel dry or damp? ... Dry. So it needs water. How much? Fill the watering can to the line — that's the right amount. Don't flood it. Plants need water, but they also need air in the soil. Water it slowly. ... Good. Now let's check again tomorrow and see if the soil is damp.
do_this: If you have planted a seed or have houseplants, make plant care a daily chore
Teach your child to check soil moisture by touch — this is sensory awareness applied to real responsibility
Connect chore to observation: "The leaves are drooping — what do you think the plant needs?"
If gardening is possible (even a pot on a windowsill), track growth: "Is it taller than yesterday? Draw what it looks like now."
daily_moments.what_this_builds: Caretaking responsibility, observation over time, cause-and-effect understanding, sensory assessment

daily_moments.say_this: Let's do a water experiment. Feel the water. Is it warm or cool? ... Now add a little cold water. What changed? ... Now tell me — when you put something in the water, what happens? Let's try this cup. Push it under. ... Bubbles! Where did those bubbles come from? That's the air that was inside the cup being pushed out by the water.
do_this: Bath time is a natural science laboratory: water temperature, floating and sinking, bubbles, pouring
Ask questions and let your child form theories: "Why does the rubber duck float but the rock sinks?"
Vocabulary: warm, cool, float, sink, pour, overflow, surface, deep, shallow
Connect to outdoor observations: "We saw water flowing downhill today at the park. Why do you think water goes downhill?"
daily_moments.what_this_builds: Curiosity, hypothesis testing, scientific vocabulary, cause-and-effect reasoning

daily_moments.say_this: Tonight let's read a story about the natural world. [Read a book about animals, plants, weather, or seasons.] ... What did you learn from that book that you didn't know before? ... What was the most interesting part? ... What do you want to look for the next time we're outside? ... Now tell me the story. ... Before you go to sleep, look out the window. Is it dark? Are there stars? What do you see?
do_this: Choose read-alouds this week that connect to the natural world: animal behavior, seasons, weather, plants growing
After reading, ask: "What do you want to observe next time we're outside?" Connect the book to future action
End with narration: have your child retell the story
Close with an observation: look out the window together. "What do you see? What do you hear?" Even the night sky is something to observe
daily_moments.what_this_builds: Nature literacy through books, observation planning, narration habit, curiosity

daily_moments.say_this: Before we go inside, let's collect our things. What did we bring out? ... The crayons, the paper, the magnifying glass, and the nature bag. Gather everything. Now — is there anything from outside that you want to bring in to look at more? Pick one thing. Leave the rest where it belongs — we don't take more than we need from nature.
do_this: Outdoor-to-indoor transition includes collecting all tools and materials
Teach environmental stewardship: take one thing, leave the rest. "We observe nature — we don't strip it."
Indoor cleanup follows the same rule: everything back where it belongs before the next activity
Ask: "Is everything put away? Check the area."
daily_moments.what_this_builds: Environmental stewardship, cleanup habit, space ownership, transition management

title: Week 7: Independence & Practical Life
parent_frame: This week, step all the way back — your child does everything they are physically capable of, on their own.
why_this_matters: The word "sovereignty" means self-rule. It begins here — not at age 14 when the student writes a rule of life, but at age three when the child pulls on their own shoes, pours their own water, and carries their own plate to the sink. Every act of genuine independence builds the identity of a person who does things for themselves. Every act of unnecessary dependence — being dressed when they could dress themselves, being carried when they could walk, having choices eliminated because it is faster — erodes that identity. In Phase 1, your child will be expected to manage their own space, complete tasks from start to finish, and solve problems before asking for help. This week, you are the parent who steps back and lets them.
focus_items: Your child does everything they are physically capable of doing independently — no help unless asked
Real kitchen participation: cracking eggs, spreading butter, pouring cereal, stirring, setting and clearing the table
Problem-solving before help: "Try it first. If you can't do it, I'll help." Give 30 seconds before stepping in
Complete tasks start to finish — not just starting things, but finishing them properly
Full self-care: toileting, hand washing, dressing, eating, bathing — all independent
"You are the kind of person who does things for yourself." Say this. Mean it.
keep_doing: Daily outdoor time · Consistent routines with decreasing prompting · Read aloud 2-3x daily · Let them do everything they can for themselves · "Please," "thank you," "excuse me" expected without prompting · Chore ownership · Cleanup before transitions
what_you_need: A step stool (for reaching sinks and counters), child-accessible dishes and utensils, a low hook for coats and bags, a small pitcher for pouring, child-safe kitchen tools (butter knife, whisk, mixing spoon), everything at your child's height that can be at your child's height

daily_moments.say_this: Good morning. You know the routine. I'm not going to say a word. You get up, you go to the bathroom, you brush your teeth, you get dressed, you make your bed, and you come to the kitchen. The whole thing. No reminders. Show me you can do it all on your own. I believe you can.
do_this: This is the test week for independence. Say nothing during the morning routine unless safety requires it
Let your child manage everything: bathroom, teeth, clothes, bed-making, arriving at breakfast ready
If they come to you with something undone, ask: "Is there anything you forgot?" Let them self-check
When they complete the entire morning independently, name it: "You did everything yourself. That is sovereignty in action. You are ready for Phase 1."
daily_moments.what_this_builds: Full morning independence, self-directed routine, self-assessment, identity as a capable person

daily_moments.say_this: You're making your own breakfast today. What do you want — cereal or toast? ... Okay, cereal. What do you need? ... Bowl, spoon, cereal, milk. Get everything out. Pour the cereal — not too much. Now the milk — pour it slowly from the small pitcher. ... Sit down, eat, and when you're done, clear your place. The whole thing is yours.
do_this: Let your child prepare their own simple meal: cereal, toast with butter, fruit with yogurt
Set up the environment so they can reach everything: cereal in a low cabinet, milk in a small pitcher, bowls accessible
Do not pour for them. Do not spread for them. Do not cut for them unless the tool is unsafe
After the meal: clear plate, wipe spot, push in chair. No reminders. If they forget, ask: "Is your spot done?"
daily_moments.what_this_builds: Kitchen independence, sequential task completion, self-service, practical life confidence

daily_moments.say_this: We're going outside. What do you need to do to get ready? ... You tell me the plan, and then you do it. I'm not going to remind you of anything. ... Good — shoes, jacket check, water bottle. Go. ... Now that we're out here, what do you want to do? You choose. You lead. I'll follow.
do_this: Let your child lead the outdoor experience. They choose the activity, the direction, the focus
If they encounter a physical challenge (a tree to climb, a stream to cross, a hill to climb), let them try before helping
If they get stuck, ask: "What could you try?" Give them time to problem-solve before offering a solution
Resist the urge to manage risk unnecessarily. A scrape from a fall is a lesson. A broken bone is not the same as a scraped knee — know the difference
daily_moments.what_this_builds: Physical risk assessment, problem-solving, self-directed activity, leadership of self

daily_moments.say_this: I see you're trying to get that puzzle piece in and it's not working. What are you going to do? ... Try turning it. Try a different spot. What other strategies do you have? ... You know what? I think you can figure this out. I'm going to give you two more minutes. If you still can't get it after you've really tried, I'll help.
do_this: This week, do not help with anything your child is physically capable of solving
When they say "I can't do it," respond: "Try it first. I'll help if you really need it."
Give them 30 seconds to one minute of effort before stepping in
When they solve something independently, name it specifically: "You figured that out yourself. Nobody helped you. That's the kind of person you are — you try first."
daily_moments.what_this_builds: Problem-solving persistence, frustration tolerance, self-efficacy, independence identity

daily_moments.say_this: Today you're going to help me make lunch. Not just one thing — the whole thing. I'm going to tell you what to do, step by step, and you're going to do each step yourself. First: wash the lettuce. Tear it into pieces and put it in the bowl. Second: get the bread out and put butter on two slices. Third: slice the banana — use the butter knife, push down firmly. Ready?
do_this: Full kitchen participation: washing, tearing, spreading, slicing (soft foods with a butter knife), stirring, pouring
Give multi-step instructions and let your child execute: "Wash the tomato, put it on the cutting board, and get me the knife"
Let them handle real food with real tools. Expect mess. The mess is the learning
When the meal is complete: "You made this. You washed it, you prepared it, you put it together. You are capable of real work."
daily_moments.what_this_builds: Kitchen competence, multi-step execution, real contribution, identity as a capable worker

daily_moments.say_this: You run the bath tonight. Test the water — is it the right temperature? Not too hot, not too cold. You decide. ... Good. Now do the whole bath yourself. I'll sit here, but I'm not going to say anything unless you ask me. When you're done, dry off, hang up the towel, and put on your pajamas. Come find me when you're completely ready for bed.
do_this: Full bath independence: running water (supervised for temperature), washing, hair care, rinsing, drying, dressing
Sit in the room for safety but do not direct. Let your child manage the entire sequence
If they skip something, let it go unless it is a hygiene concern. They will learn from experience
When they arrive fully ready for bed: "You did the entire bath and bedtime preparation on your own. That is independence."
daily_moments.what_this_builds: Complete self-care sequence, water safety awareness, independence, self-direction

daily_moments.say_this: Before we read, let's talk about what you did on your own today. What's something you did all by yourself that felt good? ... What's something that was hard to do by yourself? ... Did you figure it out? ... You're becoming the kind of person who handles things. That's what Phase 1 is all about — handling your own life and then learning amazing things on top of that. Let's read.
do_this: Reflect on the day's independence: what did they do alone? What was hard? What are they proud of?
Read a story about a character who does hard things or solves a problem on their own
After reading, narration: "Tell me that story." The narration habit is now firmly established
End with a bridge statement: "You're building the habits that Phase 1 needs. You're almost ready."
daily_moments.what_this_builds: Self-reflection, identity as a capable person, narration habit, readiness awareness

daily_moments.say_this: We're switching. You know the drill. I'm not going to say anything. Show me what you do.
do_this: Say nothing else. Watch. Does your child stop, clean up, and transition without a single reminder?
If they do, you are looking at Phase 1 readiness in action
If they need a prompt, give one — just one: "What do you need to do before we switch?"
This is the capstone: the child who transitions independently, cleans up without being told, and moves to the next activity on their own is ready
daily_moments.what_this_builds: Self-directed transition, complete independence in routine, the full IC1.1-A1 pattern
